## [00:00 - 03:02]

Did you know that an audience is going to make a decision about your credibility, your authority, your believability and your likability and any other ill-ability that there is? They're going to make these decisions about you within 30 to 60 seconds of your presentation. In this video, I'm going to share with you three remarkable ways you can start your presentation to create the best first impression possible. Let's dive into it. If you've got an MC who's introducing you as the speaker and while the MC is introducing you, the audience can see you, then understand that the audience, they're making these decisions about you way before you even get on stage. Next we've got Timotimally who should have really gone to the bathroom before the presentation but because he didn't, now he has to hold it in while he does the presentation. Of course we've got the Asian Dad Squad, never seen this one in the wild. Don't do this one. This one's really bad, really, really bad. So what should we do? Well look, the first thing is make sure you've got a good confident posture and then after that grab your hands, take him out of your pockets and put on a smile. Leave the audience good eye contact and wave at the audience, acknowledge them. And then just before I go on stage, I like to rub my hands together to show the audience I'm pumped and ready to go. The final thing I'll say here is while you're waiting for the MC to introduce you, it's absolutely okay to just have your hands by your sides. If there's no reason to use your hands, there's no need to do anything with them. Now let's get into the three remarkable ways that you can start a presentation to be able to capture the audience's attention and also to help you create the best first impression possible. Number one, storytelling. This is important for the following reason. When you story tell with an audience, you're going to have higher levels of connection. The audience is going to feel more connected to you. And an audience that is more connected to the speaker is an audience that's going to be more engaged with the speaker. Too many people start with content because they feel uneasy, they feel anxious, they feel nervous and then they feel like the only form of value that they can add as a speaker is with their content. This is not true. There are so many other different forms of value that you can add as a speaker. You can inspire an audience. You can entertain an audience and you can educate an audience. It's not just with content. That's not the only form of value. That's why storytelling is so critical because storytelling again allows you to create that really strong connection with the audience. An audience that's got a stronger connection is going to care more about the content. Now when I had this experience in the US, I was actually really scared because I was speaking in front of a military group and I knew that there were a lot of army vets in

the audience and I've never spoken in front of a military audience before nor have I ever spoken in front of any vets. So to me, I felt really nervous and anxious. And what happened was my dad saw me playing with his playing cards and practicing with them and he freaked out when he saw the back of the playing cards.
